title: Mango Chutney with Nigella Seeds

overview.description: A sweet, tangy mango chutney with ginger, warm spices, chilli and aromatic nigella seeds.

ingredients: 1kg firm, slightly underripe mangoes, peeled and diced
method: Peel the mangoes and cut the flesh into small, even pieces.
Place the mango, sugar, vinegar, ginger, garlic, chilli if using, nigella seeds, spices and salt into a large heavy-based saucepan.
Leave for 20–30 minutes if possible to allow the sugar to begin drawing moisture from the mango.
Bring the mixture slowly to a simmer.
Cook uncovered over low to medium heat, stirring regularly.
Continue cooking for approximately 60–90 minutes until the mango is tender and the chutney is thick and glossy.
Stir more frequently towards the end of cooking to prevent sticking.
The chutney should be thick enough that a spoon drawn across the bottom of the pan briefly leaves a clear trail.
Taste and adjust the balance of sweetness, acidity, salt and spice.
Spoon the chutney into hot sterilised jars while still piping hot.
Seal immediately and allow to cool undisturbed.

learning.did-you-know: Nigella seeds are sometimes called kalonji and have a complex flavour often described as a combination of onion, oregano and black pepper.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How fruit, vinegar and sugar combine to create a preserve and how aromatic seeds can add complexity without making a chutney hot.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A versatile chutney that connects naturally with the Indian dishes in the meal collection while also working beautifully with cheese and sandwiches.
chapman-tips: Use firm mangoes so the pieces retain some texture during cooking.
The mango should be ripe enough to have flavour but not so ripe that it completely dissolves.
Nigella seeds provide flavour rather than significant heat.
Adjust the chilli separately from the other spices.
The chutney will thicken further as it cools.
Don't reduce the chutney so far that it becomes sticky or jam-like.
A sharper chutney is particularly good with rich curries.
Leave the chutney to mature for at least a few days before eating if possible.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well, although properly jarred and preserved chutney is better suited to long-term storage.
notes.leftovers: Excellent with curries, cheese, sandwiches, samosas, pakoras and grilled meats.
